topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into a Tuesday afternoon. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked the link, and there it was: my piece, beautifully argued, meticulously researched, now bearing a different name. A stranger’s name. A wave of profound sadness washed over me, heavy and slow. I reread the text, the words I had poured my own heart into now belonging to someone else’s public profile. My fingers trembled slightly as I scrolled through the publication details, the realization settling in like a lead weight. I felt a sudden urge to just curl up in a quiet corner, the world seeming too loud and sharp. I started drafting an email, my voice catching in my throat as I tried to find the right words to address this bewildering injustice.

[story 2]

When the editor sent the final confirmation, the air left my lungs in a rush. It wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a fundamental erasure. I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide, slightly watering eyes. I traced the lines of the published article with a fingertip, feeling a strange disconnect, as if I were looking at a photograph of someone I knew but couldn't quite touch. A deep ache settled in my chest, a dull, persistent throb. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. I needed to talk to someone, to voice the sheer unfairness of it all, to let the weight of this misplaced authorship settle somewhere other than just inside me.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. The initial shock had worn off, replaced by a deep, quiet sorrow that seemed to seep into my bones. It was a betrayal, subtle yet devastating. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning softly under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky breath. I picked up my phone, my hands moving with a deliberate, careful slowness. The need to confront this felt overwhelming, yet strangely necessary. I began composing a message, my tone carefully modulated, striving to convey the depth of my disappointment without sounding accusatory. Every keystroke felt heavy, weighted by the understanding that someone else was now reaping the credit for my labor.
